title: Buffers
subtitle: Files in memory, navigated with :ls and :b.
summary: Vim loads each file into a buffer. :ls lists all buffers. :b switches to one by name or number. :bn / :bp step through them. :bd unloads a buffer.

blocks.text: Every file you open in Vim becomes a *buffer* — an in-memory copy. You may have ten buffers loaded but only one window showing one of them. Navigating buffers, not windows, is how you move between files.

blocks.title: What :ls Shows You
blocks.text:
{key::}{key:l}{key:s} (alias {key::}{key:b}{key:u}{key:f}{key:f}{key:e}{key:r}{key:s}) lists every buffer Vim knows about, with flags:

  • `%` — current buffer.
  • `#` — alternate (previous) buffer.
  • `+` — modified.
  • `a` — active (loaded and shown in some window).
  • `h` — hidden (loaded but not shown).
  • `-` — modifiable off.
  • `=` — read-only.

You can switch by buffer number ({key::}{key:b}{key: }{key:5}) or by name fragment ({key::}{key:b}{key: }{key:f}{key:o}{key:o}). Tab-complete works.

blocks.title: The 'hidden' Option Is Worth Setting
blocks.text:
By default Vim refuses to switch buffers if the current one is modified. Set `:set hidden` (it's on by default in Neovim) and Vim will *hide* the modified buffer instead — keeping the changes in memory while you work on another file.

Without `'hidden'`, you'll constantly hit "E37: No write since last change" and end up using {key::}{key:b}{key:!} (force) or aborting. With `'hidden'`, buffers behave like the tabs in any modern editor — switch freely, save when ready.

The trade-off is that you can {key::}{key:q} a window with unsaved buffers in the background. {key::}{key:q}{key:a}{key:l}{key:l} will warn you about them when exiting Vim.
{key:Ctrl-^} swaps to the *alternate* buffer (the one you were just in). It's the buffer-level version of {key:`}{key:`}. Most people use this dozens of times a day.
